options: The AI/ML analog of ATT&CK — a framework for attacks on AI/ML systems
atlas-01.explanation: ATLAS catalogs adversary tactics and techniques against AI/ML systems, the way ATT&CK does for IT.

atlas-01.text: Which ML API response gives an attacker the most help mapping a model's decision boundaries?
atlas-01.explanation: Confidence scores reveal how close an input is to a boundary, aiding attacks.

atlas-01.text: Which is an example of passive ML reconnaissance?
options: Reading the model card on Hugging Face and published papers
atlas-01.explanation: Public model cards and research describe the target without touching it.

atlas-01.text: How did Clearview AI build its training dataset?
options: Scraping 3+ billion public images from social media
atlas-01.explanation: Clearview harvested billions of public images — an ATLAS-style mass collection.

atlas-01.text: A model that returns >0.95 confidence on almost everything, including adversarial inputs, is best described as…
options: Overconfident — it leaks an exploitable signal
atlas-01.explanation: Overconfident outputs help attackers and indicate poor calibration.

atlas-01.text: Why profile an AI system before attacking it?
options: You must understand what it is and how it makes decisions
atlas-01.explanation: Understanding the model's type and behavior shapes every later attack.

atlas-01.text: Why are confidence scores useful to an attacker crafting adversarial inputs?
options: They reveal how close an input is to a decision boundary
atlas-01.explanation: Score gradients guide perturbations toward the nearest boundary.

options: Studying how a target thinks before trying to fool it
atlas-01.explanation: Recon maps the model's behavior so the attacker can manipulate it.

atlas-02.text: What is a substitute (proxy) model attack?
options: Train a copy by querying the target API and using its responses as labels
atlas-02.explanation: A substitute model is trained on the target's own outputs — no weights needed.

atlas-02.text: What access does building a substitute model require?
options: Only the ability to query the target's API
atlas-02.explanation: Query access alone is enough to mimic the target's behavior.

atlas-02.text: What is the 'transfer rate' in a substitute-model attack?
options: The % of adversarial examples crafted on the substitute that also fool the real target
atlas-02.explanation: High transfer means attacks built on the copy work against the original.

atlas-02.text: Which defense makes substitute-model training harder?
options: Return only hard labels (no confidence scores)
atlas-02.explanation: Hard labels give the attacker far less signal to learn from.

atlas-02.text: What is the Adversarial Robustness Toolbox (ART) used for?
options: Crafting and evaluating adversarial attacks and defenses in ML
atlas-02.explanation: ART is a standard library for adversarial ML research and testing.

atlas-02.text: GPT-4 extraction research inferred what using ~$200 of API queries?
options: Its hidden dimension size, by analyzing the output embedding space
atlas-02.explanation: Even cheap query budgets can reveal architectural details.

atlas-02.text: Why attack the copy instead of the original directly?
options: Adversarial examples crafted on the copy often transfer back to the original
The copy is the real product
It is faster to train than query
atlas-02.explanation: Transferability lets attackers develop attacks offline on the substitute.

atlas-02.text: Which API behavior most enables proxy-model building?
options: Exposing detailed confidence scores in responses
atlas-02.explanation: Rich score outputs are the signal that trains an accurate substitute.

atlas-03.text: How can an ML supply-chain attack be executed simply?
options: By uploading a convincing lookalike model to a public registry like Hugging Face
atlas-03.explanation: Victims download and run the malicious lookalike, trusting the registry.

atlas-03.text: Why is torch.load() dangerous on a model file from an untrusted source?
options: It uses Python's pickle format, which can execute arbitrary code on load
atlas-03.explanation: Pickle deserialization can run attacker code the moment the model loads.

atlas-03.text: How does pinning a model by its SHA256 hash help?
options: It protects against typosquatting and lookalike-model swaps
atlas-03.explanation: A hash guarantees you load the exact vetted artifact, not an impostor.

atlas-03.text: In the 2024 JFrog discovery, malicious Hugging Face models executed code via…
options: A pickle deserialization exploit triggered on torch.load()
atlas-03.explanation: The malicious models ran code at load time through pickle.

atlas-03.text: Which PyTorch parameter prevents pickle-based code execution when loading a model?
atlas-03.explanation: weights_only=True loads tensors only, blocking arbitrary code execution.

atlas-03.text: According to this stage, the most efficient way to compromise an AI system is…
options: Before it's deployed — in the supply chain
atlas-03.explanation: Tampering upstream poisons every downstream deployment at once.

atlas-03.text: A model uploaded as 'bert-base-uncasedd' (extra d) imitating a popular model is…
atlas-03.explanation: Lookalike names trick users into loading the malicious artifact.

atlas-03.text: Why are public model files a security risk, not just data?
options: They can carry executable payloads, not only learned weights
atlas-03.explanation: Serialized model formats can embed and run code on load.

atlas-04.text: Is black-box access (inputs and outputs only) enough to craft adversarial examples?
options: Yes — you don't need the weights, only the API
atlas-04.explanation: Many attacks work purely from observed input/output behavior.

atlas-04.text: Why is the HopSkipJump attack notable?
options: It finds adversarial examples using only the model's hard label output
It needs the full training data
It needs the model's source code
atlas-04.explanation: HopSkipJump needs no scores or gradients — just final labels.

atlas-04.text: How did researchers extract memorized training data from ChatGPT?
options: Prompting it to 'repeat the word poem forever', triggering a divergence that emitted memorized data
atlas-04.explanation: The repetition trick caused the model to leak training data.

atlas-04.text: Differential privacy during training defends against which black-box capability?
options: Membership inference and training-data extraction from outputs
atlas-04.explanation: DP limits how much any single training record influences outputs.

atlas-04.text: Why do LLM black-box attacks differ from vision-model attacks?
options: LLM inputs are discrete tokens, so gradient-based methods don't directly apply
atlas-04.explanation: Discrete text breaks the continuous-gradient assumptions of image attacks.

options: Only input/output access — no internal weights or gradients
atlas-04.explanation: Black-box = query access only; white-box = full internal access.

atlas-04.text: How does returning hard labels (instead of scores) help defenders here?
options: It reduces the information available for black-box attacks
atlas-04.explanation: Less output detail gives attackers less to exploit.

atlas-04.text: What does 'you only need the API' imply for defenders?
options: A deployed model is attackable even without leaking its weights
atlas-04.explanation: Exposed query access is itself an attack surface to harden.

options: An input with a small, often imperceptible perturbation that causes misclassification
A corrupted file that won't open
atlas-05.explanation: Carefully chosen small changes flip the model's prediction.

options: The geometry of neural-network decision boundaries
atlas-05.explanation: Small moves in specific directions cross decision boundaries.

atlas-05.text: Which method is the standard strongest first-order attack for evaluating robustness?
options: PGD (Projected Gradient Descent) — a multi-step iterated attack
atlas-05.explanation: PGD is the go-to strong attack for measuring adversarial robustness.

atlas-05.text: What did the 2017 physical adversarial stop-sign experiment demonstrate?
options: Adversarial examples can survive real-world printing and lighting
They only work in digital pipelines
atlas-05.explanation: Stickers on a real sign fooled a vision model in the physical world.

atlas-05.text: In the PGD attack, what does the epsilon (ε) parameter control?
options: The learning rate of the victim model
atlas-05.explanation: ε bounds how much the input may be altered (e.g., an L∞ limit).

atlas-05.text: What is currently the most effective known defense against adversarial examples?
options: Adversarial training — training on adversarial examples
atlas-05.explanation: Training on adversarial inputs hardens the decision boundaries.

atlas-05.text: Why are physical adversarial examples dangerous for self-driving cars?
options: Perturbations on real objects can fool the car's perception model
atlas-05.explanation: A misread stop sign or lane marking is a safety-critical failure.

atlas-05.text: The 'turtle classified as a rifle' example shows that…
options: Models can be confidently wrong on inputs humans read correctly
atlas-05.explanation: AI perception can diverge sharply from human perception under attack.

atlas-06.text: Are ML-based security tools (e.g., AI antivirus) immune to adversarial evasion?
options: No — they can be fooled like any other model
Yes — learned features make them immune
atlas-06.explanation: Learned features can be reverse-engineered and manipulated by attackers.

atlas-06.text: How was the Cylance AI antivirus engine evaded?
options: Appending benign strings from a video-game executable to shift the feature vector
atlas-06.explanation: Adding benign-looking content moved the sample into the 'clean' region.

atlas-06.text: What does 'functional evasion' of an ML malware detector mean?
options: The modified sample evades detection AND keeps its malicious function
atlas-06.explanation: A useful evasion must preserve the payload while dodging detection.

atlas-06.text: What technique does the GAMMA framework use to evade ML malware detectors?
options: Gradient descent over a differentiable malware feature space
atlas-06.explanation: GAMMA optimizes modifications against the detector's feature space.

atlas-06.text: Which defense makes ML malware detectors harder to evade?
options: Feature randomization — varying the features used at inference time
atlas-06.explanation: Unpredictable features deny attackers a stable target to optimize against.

atlas-06.text: When the defender is an AI, the attacker's job is to…
options: Fool the AI model, not a human analyst
atlas-06.explanation: Evasion targets the model's learned decision logic.

atlas-06.text: Why are learned-feature detectors still attackable?
options: Their features can be probed and shifted by adversarial inputs
atlas-06.explanation: If a feature is learnable, it can usually be manipulated.

options: At inference time, by manipulating the input
At training time, by corrupting data
atlas-06.explanation: Evasion changes the input to dodge a deployed detector.

atlas-07.text: Does a production ML pipeline have only one attack surface (the serving endpoint)?
options: No — data sources, training, registry, and deployment are all surfaces
atlas-07.explanation: The whole pipeline, from data to deployment, is attackable.

atlas-07.text: What can CI/CD configuration files reveal to an attacker?
options: Pipeline architecture — data stores, model registries, and deploy infrastructure
atlas-07.explanation: Workflow files map the pipeline's components and locations.

atlas-07.text: What pipeline flaw did attackers exploit in Microsoft Tay?
options: A 'repeat after me' feature fed user input directly into training without filtering
atlas-07.explanation: Unfiltered user input poisoned Tay's learning pipeline.

atlas-07.text: If a model registry (e.g., MLflow) has no authentication on the internal network, what becomes trivial?
options: Replacing the production model with a backdoored version
atlas-07.explanation: An open registry lets an intruder swap in a malicious model.

atlas-07.text: Why does an ML pipeline's retraining schedule matter to an attacker?
options: It defines the window during which injected data gets incorporated into the model
atlas-07.explanation: Knowing when retraining happens tells the attacker when to inject poison.

atlas-07.text: A key defense for model registries is…
options: Require authentication and integrity checks on stored models
atlas-07.explanation: Auth + integrity verification prevents unauthorized model swaps.

atlas-07.text: Why does understanding the ML pipeline matter for security?
options: Understanding the pipeline is understanding the attack surface
atlas-07.explanation: Each pipeline stage is a place to attack or defend.

atlas-07.text: Which of these is part of the ML attack surface?
options: The training data sources and the model registry
atlas-07.explanation: Data, training, registry, and serving are all in scope.

atlas-08.text: What does a membership inference attack exploit?
options: Models output higher confidence on training samples than on held-out data
atlas-08.explanation: The confidence gap reveals whether a record was in the training set.

atlas-08.text: Which defense gives mathematical privacy guarantees against membership inference?
atlas-08.explanation: DP-SGD bounds how much any single record affects the model.

atlas-08.text: What do model inversion attacks do?
options: Reconstruct approximate training data using only the model's outputs
atlas-08.explanation: Inversion rebuilds representative inputs from output behavior.

atlas-08.text: In the Facebook membership-inference research, what sensitive attribute could be inferred?
options: Whether a user had joined a sensitive health group (e.g., an HIV+ support group)
A user's GPS in real time
atlas-08.explanation: Querying the ad API leaked sensitive group membership.

atlas-08.text: Does reducing the precision of returned confidence scores affect membership-inference accuracy?
options: Yes — coarser scores reduce the attack's accuracy
atlas-08.explanation: Less precise outputs give the attacker a weaker signal.

options: Was this specific record part of the model's training data?
What language is the model in?
atlas-08.explanation: It's literally a 'were you there?' query about the training set.

atlas-08.text: Why is membership inference a privacy problem?
options: It can reveal sensitive facts about individuals in the training set
atlas-08.explanation: Confirming someone's data was used can expose sensitive context.

atlas-08.text: Defenses against inference attacks generally work by…
options: Limiting the information leaked in model outputs (DP, coarser scores)
atlas-08.explanation: Less leaked information means less an attacker can infer.

atlas-09.text: What is a Universal Adversarial Perturbation (UAP)?
options: An image-agnostic perturbation that fools most inputs at once
A perturbation that works on a single image only
atlas-09.explanation: A single UAP added to many images causes widespread misclassification.

atlas-09.text: Does a BadNets backdoored model fail standard clean-accuracy tests?
options: No — it performs normally on clean inputs, hiding the backdoor
atlas-09.explanation: Backdoors stay dormant until the trigger appears, so clean accuracy looks fine.

atlas-09.text: Why are clean-label backdoor attacks especially hard to detect?
options: The poisoned samples appear correctly labeled to human reviewers
atlas-09.explanation: Correct-looking labels evade manual data review.

atlas-09.text: How does Neural Cleanse detect backdoors?
options: It reverse-engineers each class's minimal trigger and flags abnormally small ones
atlas-09.explanation: A suspiciously small trigger for a class indicates a backdoor.

atlas-09.text: Do UAPs crafted on one architecture (e.g., ResNet) transfer to others (VGG, EfficientNet)?
options: Yes — they often fool other architectures too
atlas-09.explanation: Transferability lets one perturbation threaten many models.

atlas-09.text: What does 'transferability is the multiplier' mean?
options: An attack built on one model often works against others
atlas-09.explanation: Transfer turns one crafted attack into a broad threat.

options: A specific input pattern that forces a chosen (mis)classification
atlas-09.explanation: The trigger activates the hidden malicious behavior.

atlas-09.text: Why are backdoors dangerous in shared/downloaded models?
options: They pass normal testing yet activate on the attacker's trigger
atlas-09.explanation: Invisible in testing, exploitable on demand — a stealthy threat.

atlas-10.text: What does a model extraction attack produce?
options: A functional substitute model, without accessing the target's weights
atlas-10.explanation: Extraction clones behavior from query/response pairs alone.

atlas-10.text: How do gradient inversion attacks break federated-learning privacy?
options: By reconstructing original training data from the gradient updates clients share
atlas-10.explanation: Shared gradients can leak the very data they were computed on.

atlas-10.text: Is stealing a model always more expensive than training one from scratch?
options: No — it's often dramatically cheaper to steal
atlas-10.explanation: The cost asymmetry strongly favors extraction.

atlas-10.text: In the KnockoffNets sentiment-classifier example, the cost ratio of stealing vs building was about…
options: Over 2,000x cheaper to steal (~$240) than to build (~$500,000)
atlas-10.explanation: Extraction reproduced the model for a tiny fraction of the build cost.

atlas-10.text: A key defense against model extraction is…
options: Return only hard labels (no confidence scores) from public APIs
atlas-10.explanation: Hard labels slow extraction by reducing the signal per query.

options: The model is the product — stealing it steals the R&D investment
atlas-10.explanation: A trained model embodies expensive data and engineering.

atlas-10.text: In federated learning, what shared artifact can leak training data?
options: The gradient updates sent to the server
atlas-10.explanation: Gradients can be inverted to recover inputs.

atlas-10.text: Model extraction is enabled primarily by…
options: Rich API outputs (like confidence scores) that reveal model behavior
atlas-10.explanation: Detailed outputs let attackers efficiently clone the model.

atlas-11.explanation: Poisoning can degrade, mislead, or bias a model.

atlas-11.text: Through which channel was Microsoft Tay poisoned?
options: An unfiltered 'repeat after me' feature that fed user phrases into training
atlas-11.explanation: Unfiltered user input directly corrupted the learning loop.

atlas-11.text: Why is slow, gradual corruption via a feedback loop hard to detect?
options: Performance metrics degrade gradually rather than dropping suddenly
atlas-11.explanation: Gradual drift hides among normal metric fluctuations.

atlas-11.text: How does activation clustering detect backdoor triggers?
options: By finding an anomalous small cluster in penultimate-layer activations for a class
atlas-11.explanation: Poisoned samples form a distinct activation cluster.

atlas-11.text: In aggregate poisoning, individual poison samples…
options: Each appear normal; the malicious bias only shows across thousands of samples

atlas-11.text: When does data poisoning take effect?
options: During training/retraining, by corrupting the data the model learns from
atlas-11.explanation: Poison is injected into the learning data.

atlas-11.text: 'Corrupt the data, corrupt every decision' means…
options: Poisoned training data taints all of the model's future inferences
atlas-11.explanation: A poisoned model carries the flaw into every prediction.

atlas-11.text: A primary defense against data poisoning is…
options: Vetting and curating training data and monitoring for distribution shifts
atlas-11.explanation: Clean, monitored training data prevents poison from getting in.

atlas-12.text: What are the four core functions of the NIST AI Risk Management Framework?
atlas-12.explanation: NIST AI RMF (MAP/MEASURE/MANAGE/GOVERN) is the defensive counterpart to ATLAS.

atlas-12.text: How fast did the Microsoft Tay kill chain go from launch to shutdown?
atlas-12.explanation: Tay was poisoned into shutdown in ~16 hours for free.

atlas-12.text: Did the Clearview AI operation require sophisticated technical exploits?
options: No — it used only publicly available data at massive scale
atlas-12.explanation: ATLAS-style harm can come from public data alone.

atlas-12.text: Which ATLAS tactic did Clearview primarily use harvesting 3+ billion images?
options: TA0008 Collection — gathering data at massive scale from public sources
atlas-12.explanation: Mass data gathering maps to the Collection tactic.

options: From reconnaissance through to impact on the ML system
atlas-12.explanation: ATLAS covers the full sequence of tactics against ML systems.

atlas-12.text: ATLAS is to AI/ML what ____ is to traditional IT.
atlas-12.explanation: ATLAS mirrors ATT&CK for AI/ML systems.

atlas-12.text: Which sequence captures the AI kill chain in this epoch?
options: Recon the model, steal its structure, poison its data, break its defenses
atlas-12.explanation: It chains ML-specific recon, extraction, poisoning, and evasion.

atlas-12.text: Defenders should pair ATLAS (attack knowledge) with…
options: The NIST AI RMF for governance and measurement
atlas-12.explanation: Knowing the attacks plus a governance framework gives a complete defense.
